label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why some groups support the new law. The options are A to D. First, I need to recall the content of the article. The new law in Virginia makes first-time drunk driving offenders install ignition interlock devices if they're over the limit. The existing law only required repeat offenders or those with a higher BAC. The new law expands the use of these devices. The article mentions that groups like Mothers Against Drunk Driving and the Washington Regional Alcohol Program support this. They say Virginia still has too many alcohol-related deaths and injuries each year. They argue that the devices reduce repeat offenses. So their main point is that this will help prevent accidents and save lives. Looking at the options: A) Government can be financed to build roads - The article doesn't mention anything about funding roads. The cost is about the device, not government revenue. B) Some traffic deaths and injuries may be avoided - This matches with the groups' reasoning that the law will reduce repeat offenses, thus avoiding deaths and injuries. C) Lower-income drivers can't afford to drink again - The article actually mentions that some argue the fees will affect lower-income drivers, but this is a counter-argument, not a reason for support. Supporters probably don't think it's about making them not afford alcohol. D) Court system works more effectively - The article says the court system might be burdened,
